questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron cube (density $= 8000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, specific heat capacity $= 470 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$) is raised to a high temperature and placed upon a large block of ice at $0^\circ \text{C}$. The cube melts the ice beneath it, displaces the resulting water, and sinks. At the final equilibrium position, the upper surface of the cube just aligns with the surface of the ice. Determine the initial temperature of the cube. Neglect any heat loss outside the ice and the cube. The density of ice is $900 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$ and the latent heat of fusion of ice is $3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let the volume of the iron cube be $V$. Since the upper surface of the cube just aligns with the surface of the ice at the final equilibrium position, the volume of the ice melted is exactly equal to the volume of the cube.

Mass of the ice melted is given by:
$m_{ice} = \rho_{ice} V$

The heat required to melt this amount of ice is:
$Q = m_{ice} L_f = \rho_{ice} V L_f$

The heat released by the iron cube as it cools from its initial temperature $T$ to $0^\circ \text{C}$ is:
$Q = m_{Fe} c_{Fe} T = \rho_{Fe} V c_{Fe} T$

By the principle of calorimetry, assuming no heat loss to the surroundings, the heat lost by the iron cube equals the heat gained by the ice:
$\rho_{Fe} V c_{Fe} T = \rho_{ice} V L_f$

Solving for the initial temperature $T$:
$T = \dfrac{\rho_{ice} L_f}{\rho_{Fe} c_{Fe}}$

Substituting the given values:
$T = \dfrac{900 \times 3.36 \times 10^5}{8000 \times 470}$

$T = \dfrac{9 \times 336000}{80 \times 470}$

$T = \dfrac{302400}{3760} \approx 80.4^\circ \text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A traditional method of cooling drinking water is to keep it in a pitcher having porous walls. Water reaches the outer surface very slowly and evaporates. Most of the energy required for evaporation is extracted from the water itself, thereby cooling it down. Assume a pitcher holds $10 \text{ kg}$ of water and $0.2 \text{ g}$ of water seeps out per second. Assuming no backward heat transfer from the atmosphere to the water, determine the time in which the temperature drops by $5^\circ C$. The specific heat capacity of water is $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and the latent heat of vaporization of water is $2.27 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let $t$ be the time required for the temperature to drop by $5^\circ C$.

The rate of heat loss due to evaporation is given by:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{dm}{dt} L$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = (0.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg s}^{-1}) \times (2.27 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}) = 454 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

The total heat to be extracted from the water to cool it by $5^\circ C$ is:

$Q = M s \Delta T$

$Q = 10 \text{ kg} \times 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1} \times 5^\circ C = 210000 \text{ J}$

Equating the total heat extracted to the heat lost over time $t$:

$Q = \left(\dfrac{dQ}{dt}\right) t$

$210000 = 454 \times t$

$t = \dfrac{210000}{454} \text{ s} \approx 462.55 \text{ s}$

Converting the time into minutes:

$t = \dfrac{462.55}{60} \text{ min} \approx 7.7 \text{ min}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron piece of mass $100 \text{ g}$ is placed inside a furnace for a long duration and subsequently dropped into a calorimeter of water equivalent $10 \text{ g}$ that contains $240 \text{ g}$ of water at $20^\circ\text{C}$. The mixture reaches a final equilibrium temperature of $60^\circ\text{C}$. Determine the temperature of the furnace. The specific heat capacity of iron is $470 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let the temperature of the furnace be $T$.

Mass of the iron piece, $m_i = 100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}$
Specific heat capacity of iron, $c_i = 470 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
Total effective mass of water (water + calorimeter), $M = 240 \text{ g} + 10 \text{ g} = 250 \text{ g} = 0.25 \text{ kg}$
Specific heat capacity of water, $c_w = 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

According to the principle of calorimetry, heat lost by the iron piece equals the heat gained by the water and the calorimeter.

$m_i c_i (T - T_f) = M c_w (T_f - T_i)$

Substituting the given values:

$0.1 \times 470 \times (T - 60) = 0.25 \times 4200 \times (60 - 20)$

$47(T - 60) = 0.25 \times 4200 \times 40$

$47(T - 60) = 42000$

$T - 60 = \dfrac{42000}{47} \approx 893.6^\circ\text{C}$

$T = 893.6 + 60 = 953.6^\circ\text{C}$

The closest option is $950^\circ\text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four $2 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm}$ cubes of ice are removed from a refrigerator and placed in $200 \text{ ml}$ of a drink at $10^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the temperature of the drink once thermal equilibrium is reached. Assume that the container has negligible heat capacity and no heat is lost to the surroundings of the drink. Density of ice $= 900 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, density of the drink $= 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, specific heat capacity of the drink $= 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$, and latent heat of fusion of ice $= 3.4 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Volume of the four ice cubes is $V_{\text{ice}} = 4 \times (2 \times 2 \times 2) = 32 \text{ cm}^3 = 32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.

Mass of the ice is $m_{\text{ice}} = V_{\text{ice}} \times \rho_{\text{ice}} = 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 900 = 0.0288 \text{ kg}$.

Mass of the drink is $m_{\text{drink}} = V_{\text{drink}} \times \rho_{\text{drink}} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 0.2 \text{ kg}$.

Assuming the initial temperature of the ice is $0^\circ \text{C}$, the heat required to melt all the ice is $Q_{\text{req}} = m_{\text{ice}} \times L = 0.0288 \times 3.4 \times 10^5 = 9792 \text{ J}$.

The maximum heat that can be released by the drink in cooling from $10^\circ \text{C}$ to $0^\circ \text{C}$ is $Q_{\text{rel}} = m_{\text{drink}} \times c_{\text{drink}} \times \Delta T = 0.2 \times 4200 \times 10 = 8400 \text{ J}$.

Since $Q_{\text{rel}} < Q_{\text{req}}$, the heat released by the drink is insufficient to melt all the ice. Thus, only a fraction of the ice melts, and the mixture will consist of both ice and liquid.

Therefore, the system reaches thermal equilibrium at the melting point of ice, which is $0^\circ \text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four $2 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm}$ cubes of ice are removed from a refrigerator and placed in $200 \text{ ml}$ of a drink at $10^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the amount of ice melted, given that the ice cubes do not melt completely. Assume that the container has negligible heat capacity and no heat is lost to the surroundings of the drink. Density of ice $= 900 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, density of the drink $= 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, specific heat capacity of the drink $= 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$, and latent heat of fusion of ice $= 3.4 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Since the ice cubes do not melt completely, the final temperature of the mixture is $0^\circ \text{C}$.

Mass of the drink is given by:
$m_d = V_d \times \rho_d = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 \times 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3} = 0.2 \text{ kg}$

Heat lost by the drink in cooling from $10^\circ \text{C}$ to $0^\circ \text{C}$ is:
$Q = m_d c_d \Delta T = 0.2 \times 4200 \times (10 - 0) = 8400 \text{ J}$

Let $m$ be the mass of the ice melted. Assuming the initial temperature of the ice is $0^\circ \text{C}$, the heat gained by the ice to melt is:
$Q = m L_f = m \times 3.4 \times 10^5 \text{ J}$

By the principle of calorimetry, heat lost by the drink equals heat gained by the ice:
$m \times 3.4 \times 10^5 = 8400$

$m = \dfrac{8400}{3.4 \times 10^5} \text{ kg} = \dfrac{84}{3.4} \text{ g} \approx 24.7 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mass of $1 \text{ kg}$ of ice at $0^\circ \text{C}$ is mixed with $1 \text{ kg}$ of steam at $100^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the composition of the system once thermal equilibrium is established. The latent heat of fusion of ice is $3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$ and the latent heat of vaporization of water is $2.26 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Heat required to convert $1 \text{ kg}$ of ice at $0^\circ \text{C}$ to water at $100^\circ \text{C}$ is given by:

$Q_1 = m_{\text{ice}} L_f + m_{\text{ice}} C_w \Delta T$

$Q_1 = 1 \times 3.36 \times 10^5 + 1 \times 4200 \times 100$

$Q_1 = 3.36 \times 10^5 + 4.20 \times 10^5 = 7.56 \times 10^5 \text{ J}$

Heat released if the entire $1 \text{ kg}$ of steam at $100^\circ \text{C}$ condenses to water at $100^\circ \text{C}$:

$Q_2 = m_{\text{steam}} L_v = 1 \times 2.26 \times 10^6 = 22.6 \times 10^5 \text{ J}$

Since $Q_2 > Q_1$, the available heat from steam is more than sufficient to melt the ice and raise its temperature to $100^\circ \text{C}$. Thus, the final temperature of the mixture will be $100^\circ \text{C}$ and only a fraction of the steam will condense.

Let $m$ be the mass of steam that condenses to provide the required heat $Q_1$:

$m L_v = Q_1$

$m \times 2.26 \times 10^6 = 7.56 \times 10^5$

$m = \dfrac{7.56 \times 10^5}{2.26 \times 10^6} \approx 0.3345 \text{ kg} = 334.5 \text{ g}$

The mass of steam remaining in the mixture:

$m_{\text{remaining steam}} = 1000 \text{ g} - 334.5 \text{ g} = 665.5 \text{ g} \approx 665 \text{ g}$

The total mass of water in the mixture is the initial mass of ice plus the mass of condensed steam:

$m_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ g} + 334.5 \text{ g} = 1334.5 \text{ g} \approx 1.335 \text{ kg}$

The final composition of the system is $665 \text{ g}$ of steam and $1.335 \text{ kg}$ of water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An aluminium vessel of mass $0.5 \text{ kg}$ holds $0.2 \text{ kg}$ of water at $20^\circ\text{C}$. A block of iron of mass $0.2 \text{ kg}$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$ is gently placed into the water. Determine the equilibrium temperature of the mixture. The specific heat capacities of aluminium, iron, and water are $910 \text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, $470 \text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, and $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, respectively.
questions.solution:
Using the principle of calorimetry, heat lost by the hot body is equal to the heat gained by the cold bodies.

Let the equilibrium temperature of the mixture be $T$.

Heat lost by the iron block:
$Q_{Fe} = m_{Fe} c_{Fe} (100 - T)$
$Q_{Fe} = 0.2 \times 470 \times (100 - T) = 94 (100 - T)$

Heat gained by the aluminium vessel and water:
$Q_{Al} + Q_{w} = m_{Al} c_{Al} (T - 20) + m_{w} c_{w} (T - 20)$
$Q_{Al} + Q_{w} = (0.5 \times 910 + 0.2 \times 4200) (T - 20)$
$Q_{Al} + Q_{w} = (455 + 840) (T - 20) = 1295 (T - 20)$

Equating heat lost to heat gained:
$94 (100 - T) = 1295 (T - 20)$
$9400 - 94 T = 1295 T - 25900$
$1389 T = 35300$
$T = \dfrac{35300}{1389} \approx 25.4^\circ\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the time required to heat $20 \text{ kg}$ of water from $10^\circ C$ to $35^\circ C$ using an immersion heater rated at $1000 \text{ W}$. Assume that $80\%$ of the power input is utilized to heat the water. Specific heat capacity of water $= 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The heat required to raise the temperature of the water is given by:

$Q = mc\Delta T$

Substituting the given values:

$Q = 20 \times 4200 \times (35 - 10)$

$Q = 20 \times 4200 \times 25 = 2100000 \text{ J}$

The useful power utilized to heat the water is $80\%$ of the rated power:

$P_{useful} = \dfrac{80}{100} \times 1000 = 800 \text{ W}$

The time $t$ required to heat the water is:

$t = \dfrac{Q}{P_{useful}} = \dfrac{2100000}{800} = 2625 \text{ s}$

Converting the time into minutes:

$t = \dfrac{2625}{60} \text{ min} = 43.75 \text{ min}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a winter day, the tap water temperature is $20^\circ\text{C}$ while the room temperature is $5^\circ\text{C}$. Water is stored in a tank of capacity $0.5 \text{ m}^3$ for household use. If it were possible to utilize the heat liberated by the water to vertically lift a $10 \text{ kg}$ mass, how high can it be lifted as the water reaches room temperature? Take $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
Volume of water, $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$

Mass of water, $M = V \rho = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ kg}$

Heat liberated by water as it cools from $20^\circ\text{C}$ to $5^\circ\text{C}$ is $Q = M s \Delta T$

Substituting the standard specific heat of water $s = 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, we get:

$Q = 500 \times 4200 \times (20 - 5) = 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$

This heat is utilized to lift a mass $m = 10 \text{ kg}$ to a height $h$. The work done is equal to the increase in potential energy.

$mgh = Q$

$10 \times 10 \times h = 3.15 \times 10^7$

$100 h = 3.15 \times 10^7$

$h = 3.15 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 315 \text{ km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A brick having a mass of $4.0 \text{ kg}$ is dropped from a height of $2.0 \text{ m}$ into a river that is $1.0 \text{ m}$ deep. Assuming $80\%$ of the gravitational potential energy is ultimately transformed into thermal energy, determine this thermal energy in calories.
questions.solution:
Total height fallen by the brick is $h = 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}$

Loss in gravitational potential energy is $\Delta U = mgh$

Taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, we get $\Delta U = 4.0 \times 10 \times 3.0 = 120 \text{ J}$

Thermal energy produced is $80\%$ of the loss in potential energy

$E = 0.80 \times 120 = 96 \text{ J}$

Converting joules to calories using $1 \text{ cal} \approx 4.2 \text{ J}$

$E = \dfrac{96}{4.2} \approx 22.85 \text{ cal}$

The closest value among the options is $23 \text{ cal}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bullet of mass $20 \text{ g}$ enters a fixed wooden block with a speed of $40 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ and comes to rest inside it. Determine the change in internal energy during the process.
questions.solution:
The initial kinetic energy of the bullet is given by $K_{i} = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$.

Substituting the given values, $m = 20 \text{ g} = 0.02 \text{ kg}$ and $v = 40 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$K_{i} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.02 \times (40)^{2}$

$K_{i} = 0.01 \times 1600 = 16 \text{ J}$

The final kinetic energy of the bullet is $K_{f} = 0$ since it comes to rest.

According to the law of conservation of energy, the loss in kinetic energy is converted into the internal energy of the system.

Change in internal energy $\Delta U = K_{i} - K_{f} = 16 \text{ J} - 0 = 16 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $50 \text{ kg}$ man is running at a speed of $18 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. If the entirety of the man's kinetic energy is utilized to raise the temperature of water from $20^\circ C$ to $30^\circ C$, what mass of water can be heated with this energy?
questions.solution:
Velocity of the man, $v = 18 \text{ km h}^{-1} = 18 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m s}^{-1} = 5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Kinetic energy of the man, $K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$

$K = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times (5)^2 = 625 \text{ J}$

This kinetic energy is utilized to heat the water. The heat energy required is given by $Q = m_w s \Delta T$

Given $\Delta T = 30^\circ\text{C} - 20^\circ\text{C} = 10^\circ\text{C}$ and specific heat of water $s \approx 4.18 \text{ J g}^{-1} ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

Equating kinetic energy to heat energy:

$625 = m_w \times 4.18 \times 10$

$m_w = \dfrac{625}{41.8} \text{ g} \approx 14.95 \text{ g}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the mass of water that can be heated is $15 \text{ g}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three different liquids $A$, $B$, and $C$ have temperatures of $12^\circ \text{C}$, $19^\circ \text{C}$, and $28^\circ \text{C}$ respectively, for equal masses. The temperature when $A$ and $B$ are mixed is $16^\circ \text{C}$, while mixing $B$ and $C$ yields a temperature of $23^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the temperature when $A$ and $C$ are mixed.
questions.solution:
Let the specific heat capacities of liquids $A$, $B$, and $C$ be $s_A$, $s_B$, and $s_C$ respectively. Let the mass of each liquid be $m$.

When liquids $A$ and $B$ are mixed, the equilibrium temperature is $16^\circ \text{C}$. By the principle of calorimetry, heat gained by $A$ equals heat lost by $B$:

$m s_A (16 - 12) = m s_B (19 - 16)$

$4 s_A = 3 s_B \Rightarrow s_A = \dfrac{3}{4} s_B$

When liquids $B$ and $C$ are mixed, the equilibrium temperature is $23^\circ \text{C}$. Heat gained by $B$ equals heat lost by $C$:

$m s_B (23 - 19) = m s_C (28 - 23)$

$4 s_B = 5 s_C \Rightarrow s_C = \dfrac{4}{5} s_B$

Let $T$ be the equilibrium temperature when liquids $A$ and $C$ are mixed. Heat gained by $A$ equals heat lost by $C$:

$m s_A (T - 12) = m s_C (28 - T)$

Substituting the values of $s_A$ and $s_C$ in terms of $s_B$:

$\dfrac{3}{4} s_B (T - 12) = \dfrac{4}{5} s_B (28 - T)$

$\dfrac{3}{4} (T - 12) = \dfrac{4}{5} (28 - T)$

Multiplying both sides by $20$:

$15(T - 12) = 16(28 - T)$

$15T - 180 = 448 - 16T$

$31T = 628$

$T = \dfrac{628}{31} \approx 20.258^\circ \text{C}$

Rounding to one decimal place, the temperature is $20.3^\circ \text{C}$.
